name: architecture
context: Runtime architecture — examples/ is source of truth, server indexes for search, web renders trees
info: Explains how the Dojo server and web app load ecosystems, build the search index, serve knowledge endpoints, handle backlink resolution, and render skill trees. Essential reading when adding new ecosystems, debugging search results, understanding why backlinks or aliases resolve (or don't), or diagnosing why a node does or doesn't appear in the UI.
triggers: why is my dojo node not in search
what is dojo source of truth
how does the dojo ui load ecosystems
how does dojo search indexing work
body:
# Dojo Architecture

The registry server walks `examples/` recursively and loads every `skill.json` file into an in-memory store. Everything downstream — search, trees, knowledge endpoints, the web UI — reads from this store.

## Data flow

```
examples/            server boot           API
  ├── ethereum/  ──► parse & validate ──► /v1/tree/ethereum
  ├── aws/       ──► build store      ──► /v1/search?q=...
  ├── dojo/      ──► build index      ──► /v1/learn/dojo/spec
  └── ...        ──► compute backlinks──► /v1/backlinks/...
                          │
                          ▼
                     web app
                     /v1/ecosystems ──► sidebar roots
                     /v1/tree/*     ──► lazy subtrees
                     /v1/learn/*    ──► knowledge view
```

## Key invariant

**`examples/` is the single source of truth.** The file `registry/index.json` exists for external discovery tools and should stay in sync, but the server ignores it at runtime. If a node exists in `examples/` with a valid `uri` and `parent`, it's live. If it's only in `index.json`, it's invisible.

## Boot sequence

1. Walk `examples/` recursively, find every `skill.json`
2. Parse each manifest, validate `uri` and `parent` references
3. Build parent-child edges into an in-memory tree
4. Build the search index from all text fields
5. Compute backlinks by inverting `links` and `related` arrays
6. Start the HTTP server

If a manifest has a `parent` URI that doesn't match any loaded node, the server logs a warning and skips the node. Fix broken parents before deploying.

sections.title: Runtime source of truth
sections.body:
The server trusts `examples/` exclusively. This means:

- A node in `examples/` with valid `uri` and `parent` **will** appear in the API, even if `registry/index.json` doesn't mention it.
- A node listed in `index.json` but missing from `examples/` **will not** appear in the API.
- Renaming a directory in `examples/` without updating the `uri` inside the manifest will break the node.
- Deleting a directory removes the node from the next server restart.

Keep `registry/index.json` in sync for external tools, but don't treat it as authoritative.

sections.title: Search index
sections.body:
The search index is built at boot from these manifest fields:

| Field | Indexed? | Weight | Notes |
|-------|----------|--------|-------|
| `context` | Yes | High | Primary routing text |
| `info` | Yes | Medium | Longer description |
| `tags` | Yes | High | Exact-match boost |
| `aliases` | Yes | High | Alternate names |
| `triggers` | Yes | Medium | Natural-language phrases |
| `body` | Yes | Low | Long-form content |
| `sections[].body` | Yes | Low | Section content |
| `sections[].tags` | Yes | Medium | Granular section tags |
| `name`, `uri` | Yes | Medium | Identity |

Fields that are empty or missing are silently skipped — they don't contribute to search ranking. This means a manifest with only `context` and `tags` is findable but will rank lower than one with rich `aliases`, `body`, and `sections`.

**Re-indexing:** The index is built once at boot. After editing manifests, restart the server to pick up changes. There is no hot-reload yet.

sections.title: Backlink resolution
sections.body:
Backlinks are computed at boot by scanning every loaded manifest:

1. For each node, iterate its `links` array — each `{uri}` entry creates a backlink from the target to the source.
2. For each node, iterate its `related` array — each `{uri, relation}` entry creates a typed backlink.
3. Store the inverted index in memory.

The `/v1/backlinks/<uri>` endpoint returns all source nodes that link to or relate to the given URI. This is how the knowledge UI shows "What links here?" for any node.

**Common issue:** If you add `links` pointing to a node but that node's backlinks page is empty, restart the server — backlinks are computed at boot, not on the fly.

sections.title: Alias resolution
sections.body:
At boot, the server builds an alias-to-URI map from every node's `aliases` array. The `/v1/alias/:alias` endpoint looks up this map and returns the canonical `uri`.

Rules:
- Aliases are case-insensitive for lookup
- If two nodes share the same alias, the first one loaded wins (load order is filesystem-dependent — avoid collisions)
- Use specific aliases: `"ERC-20 transfer"` is better than `"transfer"` to avoid shadowing other ecosystems
- The agent endpoint `/v1/agent/learn` also uses alias resolution when a query matches an alias better than a keyword search

sections.title: Knowledge routes
sections.body:
**`GET /v1/learn/<uri>`** — Renders `body` + `sections` for a single node. Returns the full knowledge payload: title, body (markdown), sections array, links, related, and frontmatter. This is the primary endpoint for agent learning.

**`GET /v1/backlinks/<uri>`** — Returns all nodes whose `links` or `related` point at this URI. Each result includes the source node's `uri`, `context`, and the relationship type (if from `related`).

**`GET /v1/graph/<uri>`** — Returns the local neighborhood: parent, children, outgoing links, outgoing related, incoming backlinks. This is the endpoint agents use to explore the knowledge graph around a concept.

**`GET /v1/alias/:alias`** — Resolves an alias string to the canonical URI. Returns `{uri, name, context}` or 404.

**`POST /v1/agent/learn`** — Structured query interface for agents. Accepts a natural-language question, searches across all indexed fields and aliases, and returns the most relevant node's knowledge payload.

sections.title: Ecosystems endpoint
sections.body:
**`GET /v1/ecosystems`** returns all loaded nodes with `type: ecosystem`. The web app uses this to populate the sidebar with top-level roots.

New ecosystems appear automatically once their `skill.json` lands in `examples/` and the server restarts. There's no separate registration step.

The response includes each ecosystem's `uri`, `name`, `context`, `tags`, and child count. The web app fetches full subtrees lazily via `/v1/tree/<uri>` as the user navigates.

sections.title: Web rendering model
sections.body:
The web app is a single-page application that talks exclusively to the API — it never reads from the filesystem.

**Navigation flow:**
1. On load, fetch `/v1/ecosystems` to get roots for the sidebar
2. When user clicks an ecosystem, fetch `/v1/tree/<uri>` for the subtree
3. When user clicks a node, fetch `/v1/learn/<uri>` for the knowledge view
4. Render `body` as markdown, `sections` as expandable blocks, `links` as clickable references, `related` as typed badges
5. Show backlinks panel from `/v1/backlinks/<uri>` — "What links here?"

**Search:** The search bar queries `/v1/search?q=...` and displays results ranked by relevance. Rich manifests (with `aliases`, `body`, `sections`) rank higher than thin ones.

sections.title: Adding a new ecosystem
sections.body:
To make a new top-level ecosystem visible:

1. Create `examples/<name>/skill.json` with `type: ecosystem` and `parent: null`
2. Add at least `name`, `version`, `uri`, `type`, `context`, `info`, `tags`
3. Optionally add child nodes as subdirectories (standards, skills, context nodes)
4. Restart the server
5. Verify: `curl localhost:3000/v1/ecosystems | jq '.[].uri'` should include your new ecosystem
6. Open a PR per [[dojo/contributing]]

The ecosystem will appear in the web UI sidebar automatically after merge and deploy.

sections.title: Deploy cycle
sections.body:
The current deploy model:

1. PR merges to `main`
2. CI builds and deploys the server
3. Server boots, walks `examples/`, builds store + index + backlinks
4. New/updated nodes are live

There is no incremental index update yet — every deploy rebuilds the full index. For the current tree size (~50 nodes) this takes under a second. If the tree grows to thousands of nodes, incremental indexing may be needed.

To verify a deploy, check `/v1/learn/<uri>` for your node in production.

sections.title: Troubleshooting
sections.body:
**Node doesn't appear in `/v1/tree`.** Check that `parent` matches an existing node's `uri` exactly. Check the server logs for "parent not found" warnings.

**Node doesn't appear in search.** Populated fields are indexed; empty fields aren't. Add `tags`, `aliases`, or `body` with your search terms. Restart the server to re-index.

**Backlinks page is empty.** Backlinks are computed from other nodes' `links` and `related` arrays. If no one links to your node yet, the backlinks page will be empty. Also: restart after adding links — backlinks are computed at boot.

**Alias returns 404.** Check spelling and case. If two nodes share the same alias, only one wins. Use more specific aliases.

**Web UI shows stale data.** The web app caches API responses. Hard-refresh the browser or clear the cache. If the API itself is stale, the server needs a restart/redeploy.
